examples.title: Agile Iteration Lifecycle
examples.description: Demonstrates how Agile manages cost of change through iterative feedback loops
examples.code:
AGILE ITERATION PROCESS
BEGIN
 WHILE project_not_complete DO
 PLAN: Define sprint goals and select backlog items
 EXECUTE: Develop features and write tests
 REVIEW: Demo working increment to stakeholders
 RETROSPECT: Reflect on what worked and what didn't
 ADAPT: Update product backlog based on feedback
 
 // Cost of change is low here because:
 // - Changes based on fresh feedback
 // - Defects caught early in iteration
 // - Requirements still flexible
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Each iteration is a mini-project that produces working software. The feedback loop allows course correction while costs are still manageable, keeping the cost curve flatter than traditional methods.

examples.title: Test-Driven Development (TDD) - Reducing Change Costs
examples.description: Shows how writing tests first reduces defect detection costs
examples.explanation: TDD ensures defects are caught at the moment of creation when they are cheapest to fix, significantly reducing the overall cost of change throughout the project lifecycle.

examples.title: Continuous Integration Pipeline
examples.description: Demonstrates automated testing pipeline that reduces integration defect costs
examples.code:
CI/CD PIPELINE
BEGIN
 WHEN developer commits code THEN
 TRIGGER: Automated build process
 RUN: Unit tests
 RUN: Integration tests
 RUN: Code quality checks
 
 IF all tests pass THEN
 DEPLOY: To staging environment
 RUN: Acceptance tests
 IF acceptance passes THEN
 DEPLOY: To production
 END IF
 ELSE
 NOTIFY: Team of failure
 FIX: Issues immediately
 END IF
 END WHEN
 
 // Benefits:
 // - Integration issues detected immediately
 // - Changes are small and easier to fix
 // - Fast feedback prevents defect accumulation
END
examples.explanation: Continuous Integration catches integration problems immediately when they occur, preventing the exponential cost growth that happens when issues are discovered late in testing or production.

examples.title: Cost of Change Calculation Model
examples.description: Model demonstrating how costs increase exponentially across project phases
examples.code:
CALCULATE_CHANGE_COST
INPUT: change_type, current_phase
BEGIN
 base_costs = {
 requirements: 1,
 design: 5,
 implementation: 20,
 testing: 50,
 maintenance: 100
 }
 
 // Exponential factor based on how far the change must propagate
 IF current_phase == requirements THEN
 cost = base_costs[change_type] * 1
 ELSE IF current_phase == design THEN
 cost = base_costs[change_type] * 3
 ELSE IF current_phase == implementation THEN
 cost = base_costs[change_type] * 10
 ELSE IF current_phase == testing THEN
 cost = base_costs[change_type] * 25
 ELSE IF current_phase == maintenance THEN
 cost = base_costs[change_type] * 50
 END IF
 
 RETURN cost
 
 // Agile keeps costs lower because:
 // - More iterations mean earlier phase detection
 // - Continuous testing catches issues before next phase
 // - Modular architecture limits change propagation
END
examples.explanation: This model demonstrates the exponential nature of change costs. In Agile, the iterative nature and continuous testing help catch changes in earlier 'phases' within each sprint, maintaining lower costs overall.
